english_query: why sharing all you know is not always the best move
ugf_response: First, we think that if we tell other people what we know, they can use those facts to help them. Then we see that sometimes the other people already have their own facts that work for them, and hearing our facts can make them doubt or change the good ways they already use. This can cause them to waste time or even make a mistake. Next, we notice that some facts can be very private or can hurt a person if they are told to the wrong group, so keeping them to yourself can keep that person safe. After that, we understand that when a group is trying to make a plan, too many voices can make the group slow down or argue, and the best plan can be lost in the noise. Finally, we see that the smartest choice is to look at each situation, think about who will need the facts, how the facts will be used, and whether telling them will bring more good than more trouble, because sharing everything is not always the best move.

english_query: strict conditional, denying_antecedent — navigation_and_travel
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is true is a rule that links one action to another. They say that if a person does a certain thing, then a certain result will happen. In plain words they are saying: if a person uses a map to find a way, then that person will end up on the right road. This is presented as a fact that they think is correct.

The second idea that follows from the first is a step that the person takes when they notice that the first part of the rule is not true for a particular case. They look at a situation where a person does not use a map, and then they say that because the first part is not true, the second part must also not be true. In plain words they think: the person did not use a map, so the person will not end up on the right road. This is the conclusion they draw and they treat it as something that must happen.

When we look at the thinking that leads from the first idea to the second, we see that it is not a good step. The rule only tells us that doing the first thing guarantees the second thing, but it does not tell us that not doing the first thing guarantees not getting the second thing. In other words, the rule works in one direction only. To say that the opposite direction must also be true is a mistake. The thinking takes a one‑way link and treats it as if it were a two‑way link, and that is not a reliable way to decide what will happen. So while the first idea may be true, the step that says the second idea follows from it is not a good piece of reasoning.

english_query: what it means to live a good life by being a good person
ugf_response:
The first idea that the case says is that a person who does kind and fair things is a good person and that a life that feels happy and safe is a good life. The case tells us that the good parts of a life come from the good parts of the person who lives it. The second idea that the case then says is that because being a good person makes a life good, we should try to be a good person if we want a good life. It also says that if we are good then the life we have will be good, so the good life follows from the good person.

When we look at the thinking, we see that the move from the first idea to the second idea is only part of the whole picture. It is true that kind and fair actions can help a life feel better, and it is also true that many people think a good life is a life that feels happy and safe. But there are other things that also help a life be good, such as health, luck, money, and the way other people act. Because the case does not talk about those other things, the step from the first idea to the second idea is not a full proof that being a good person is the only way to get a good life.

So the thinking in the case is partly right, because being kind and fair does often make a life feel better, but it is not enough on its own. A better way to think would be to add that a good life comes from many parts, including kind actions, good health, luck, and the way other people treat us. In that sense the case has a good start, but the thinking needs more pieces to be fully strong.

english_query: counterexample to: A person is temperate if and only if (i) he moderates his appetites, (ii) he doe
ugf_response: First, the first idea says that a person is calm in feeling exactly when he does two things: he keeps his wants for food and drink in the middle and he does not let those wants lead him to act. Second, the second idea gives a case of a person who keeps his wants in the middle and does not let them lead him to act, but who still feels angry or not calm, and also a case of a person who is calm but does not keep his wants in the middle or does let them lead him to act. This case is used to show that the first idea does not work for every person. Third, if the first idea were true then every person who keeps his wants in the middle and does not let them lead him to act would be calm, and every calm person would keep his wants in the middle and not let them lead him to act. The case shows that this does not happen, so the claim that the two things are exactly the same as being calm is not always true. The thinking that the claim follows directly from the two things is not good because it ignores other things that can affect calm feeling.

english_query: how paying close attention to what someone needs is a kind of good
ugf_response:
Person 1: I hear that looking close at what someone needs is a kind of good. What does that mean?  
Person 2: It means when you watch what a person wants or feels, and you try to give it, you are doing something nice for them.  
Person 1: Why is that called a good thing?  
Person 2: Because it makes the other person feel safe and happy, and it helps them get what they need.  
Person 1: How does that help the person who is watching?  
Person 2: When you help, you also feel good inside, and people may trust you more.  
Person 1: Can you give a simple example?  
Person 2: If a friend is cold and you notice and give them your warm shirt, you are using your look and care to meet a need, and that is a good act.  
Person 1: So it creates a round of nice feeling for both?  
Person 2: Yes, the more you watch and help, the more good feeling you get, and the more people want to do the same.

english_query: how to decide when the right thing to do is not clear
ugf_response:
The first idea that the case puts forward is that when we do not have a clear sign of what the right thing to do is, we should first admit that we do not know. This claim is true in many situations because the world often holds many pieces of information that we have not yet seen or that are hard to see at all. The second idea says that because we admit we do not know, the next step is to look for more facts, to ask other people, or to think about similar past cases. This step follows from the first idea in a sensible way: if we see that we lack a clear sign, it makes sense to try to get more facts before we act. The third idea says that after we have gathered more facts we should pick the action that seems to bring the most good feeling to the most people, or that seems the least likely to hurt anyone. This also follows from the second idea, because having more facts gives us a better chance to see what might help or hurt.

When we check whether each idea is true, the first idea holds up because it simply states a fact about our limited view. The second idea also holds up in the sense that looking for more facts or asking others is a real way people often try to solve a puzzle. The third idea is true in many cases, but it can be false if the facts we gather are not enough to see all the ways people might feel, or if the feeling of most people is not the only thing we care about.

Looking at the thinking that moves from one idea to the next, the move from the first idea to the second idea is a good step. It takes a simple admission of not knowing and turns it into an action that can give us more information, which is a clear and useful way to act. The move from the second idea to the third idea is also a reasonable step, but it depends on the belief that the best action is the one that makes most people feel good. That belief can be a good guide in many everyday situations, yet it may not work well when the feelings of a few are very strong or when other values matter more. So the overall chain of thought works well as a general guide, but it is not a perfect rule for every single case.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premisses and a
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is that a line of thought is good only when it has a group of start ideas and a final idea and also when there is no way to find a situation where the start ideas are all true but the final idea is not true. The words they use to describe this are simple: the line of thought must be made of some start ideas, must end in one end idea, and must never be able to be shown wrong by any example where the start ideas hold and the end idea fails.

The second idea they try to show is a special case that they think shows the first idea is not always true. They give a line of thought that does have a group of start ideas and a final idea, and they point to a situation where the start ideas are true and the final idea is also true, but they claim that there is another situation where the start ideas are true and the final idea is still true, so they say this does not break the rule. Then they say there is a different situation where the start ideas are true and the final idea is false, and they say this shows the rule does not work.

The third idea is the judgment about whether the second idea really shows a problem. To decide this we must check if the special case really does have a situation where all the start ideas are true while the final idea is false. If such a situation exists, then the line of thought would not be good according to the first idea, and the special case would be a real problem. If no such situation exists, then the special case does not break the rule and the first idea stays safe. Looking at the details, the person’s example does not actually give a clear situation where the start ideas are true and the final idea is false; they only talk about a situation where both are true. Because of that, the claim that the example is a problem is weak. The thinking goes from the first idea to the second idea by saying “if I can find one case where the start ideas are true and the final idea is false, then the rule is broken.” That step is correct, but the example they chose does not give that case, so the reasoning does not succeed. In short, the first idea is a true description of what makes a line of thought good, the second idea tries to say it is false, but the third idea shows the attempt does not work because the example does not actually meet the needed condition. The overall thinking is therefore not good, because the conclusion does not follow from the example given.

english_query: indicative conditional, modus_ponens — predicate
ugf_response: The first idea is a sentence that says if one thing is true then another thing is true. It looks like “if A happens then B will happen.” The second idea is what the speaker says they can get from that first sentence. They say that because A has happened, we can be sure that B will happen. They are using the fact that the first part of the if‑then sentence is true to say that the second part must also be true. The way they think about it is a good way to think, because when an if‑then sentence is true and the first part of it is true, the second part does follow. In other words, seeing the first part true lets us move to the second part without any extra steps, so the thinking is sound and helps us reach a correct result.

english_query: counterexample to: A person P acquires title to land by prescription if and only if (i) P has been
ugf_response:
The first part of the case is a claim about a rule. The claim says that a person gets a right to a piece of ground when they have used that piece for a long time, and that the right only comes if they have used it for a long time. The person who makes the claim also says that there are other things that must happen, such as the person using the ground openly and without being stopped, and the use lasting for a long stretch of years. The claim is presented as a true description of when a person can get that right.

The second part of the case is what the speaker thinks follows from that claim. They say that because the claim sets out those conditions, any situation that does not meet all of them must not give a person a right to the ground. They point to a situation where a person has used the ground for a long time but one of the other conditions is missing, and they say that this shows the rule does not work in that case. In other words, they think that the missing condition proves the rule is wrong, because the rule says all the conditions are needed.

The third part is an evaluation of that thinking. The thinking is clear in that it follows the rule’s own wording: if a rule says all the parts must be there, then a case that lacks one part will not meet the rule. However, the step that calls the whole rule wrong because of one missing part may not be fully fair. A rule can still be right for the cases it was meant to cover, even if there are other cases that do not fit. So the idea that one example that does not fit means the whole rule is false is a weak jump. The thinking shows a good grasp of the rule’s details, but the conclusion that the rule is false needs more support than just a single case that falls outside the rule’s intended range.

english_query: the problem with thinking you know more than you really do
ugf_response: First, we see that a person can think they have the right answer and feel sure they know more than they really do. Then we see that the person may not have all the true things they need to be right and may act in a way that is not right. When they act, other people can see this and may copy it, and the wrong idea can spread. Also, the person may not ask for help or look for more true things because they think they already know enough, and this stops them from learning more. In the end, the result is that the person stays with a wrong idea and may cause other people to stay with it too.

english_query: how the kind of person you are matters more than following rules
ugf_response: First, we notice that a person can have a way of being that is kind, caring, honest and that stays even when no one tells them what to do. Then we see that there is a list of things that say what you should do and that many people are asked to follow that list. This means that the list can tell you to act in a certain way even if it does not fit who you are inside. Next, we think that if a person follows the list but does not feel kind or caring, the result can hurt other people. This shows that being a kind, caring, honest person can lead to good actions even when the list does not ask for them. Then we notice that a person who is kind will try to do what feels right for others, even if the list says something else. This means the way a person is inside can be stronger than the list of what to do. Finally, we understand that the best way for many people to live together is to have many kind, caring people, because they will act in good ways even without the list telling them, while just following the list without a good heart can still cause hurt.

english_query: material conditional, modus_tollens — weather_and_planning
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being used says that if it will rain then we will cancel the picnic. In this case the speaker is taking that statement to be true, meaning they accept that the rain will always make them stop the picnic.

The second idea that follows from the first is that because the picnic is not cancelled we can say that it will not rain. This step is the way of thinking that looks at the second part, sees it is not true, and then says the first part must also not be true.

The thinking behind the second idea works only if the first idea is truly always true and if there are no other reasons that could make the picnic be cancelled. In real life there can be many other reasons to cancel, such as a problem with food or a change in who can come, so the step from the picnic not being cancelled to the rain not happening is not always reliable. The argument is therefore only as good as the certainty of the first idea and the absence of any other cause for cancellation.

english_query: counterexample to: A set of sentences is consistent if and only if there exists a model that satisf
ugf_response:
The first idea that the speaker talks about is a claim that a group of words can be called “without clash” if and only if there is some possible situation in which every word in the group comes out true. In other words, the speaker says that the only way a group of words can avoid any clash is when we can picture a world where all of them are true at the same time, and that if we can picture such a world then the group must be without clash.

The second idea is that the speaker then gives a special case that they say shows the claim does not work. They pick a group of words that look like they have no clash when we look at them one by one, but then they say that we cannot find any possible situation that makes all of them true together. They use this special case as a way to say that the claim that “without clash” always means “there is a world where all are true” must be wrong.

The thinking that follows from the second idea is that because the special case seems to break the claim, the whole claim must be false. This step is only good if the special case really does show a real break. If the special case is built correctly and truly cannot be made true in any world, then the reasoning is strong and the original claim would indeed be false. However, if the special case actually does have a hidden way to be true in some world, or if the way the speaker decided what counts as “without clash” is not the same as the usual meaning, then the step from the special case to the conclusion is weak. In that situation the speaker’s thinking would not be good, because they would be using a mistaken example to knock down a claim that might still be right. So the quality of the reasoning depends on whether the example truly shows a break; if it does, the reasoning is solid, and if it does not, the reasoning is flawed.

english_query: counterexample to: Perception guides action if and only if (i) S has a conscious perceptual experie
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is a claim that a person’s seeing of something only leads to a later move or choice when that person is also aware of what they are seeing. In other words, they say that if a person does not feel that they are seeing, then the seeing does not help guide what the person does. The second idea they bring up is a story about a person who, without feeling that they are seeing, still makes a move that matches what they are seeing. For example, they describe a person who walks into a dark room, bumps into a wall, and then turns away, even though the person says they did not feel that they saw the wall. This story is presented as a case that shows the first idea may be wrong. The third idea they draw from the story is that because this case exists, the original claim that seeing only guides action when a person is aware of the seeing must be false. They think that one single case is enough to knock down the whole claim.

Looking at the reasoning, the step from the second idea to the third idea is a big jump. A single case can show that a claim is not always true, but it does not automatically prove that the claim is never true. It might be that the person in the story was actually aware of the seeing in a way that they could not put into words, or that another factor, such as a reflex, caused the move. The reasoning would be stronger if they showed many different cases, or if they explained why the person truly had no awareness at all. As it stands, the thinking moves quickly from “here is one case” to “the whole claim must be wrong,” which is a weak step. It points out a possible problem with the claim, but it does not fully settle whether the claim is always wrong.

english_query: probabilistic conditional, hypothetical_syllogism — criminal
ugf_response:
The first idea that the speaker says is a claim about what is true. They say that if a person does one act that can be seen as a sign of breaking a rule, then it is likely that the same person will also do another act that is also a sign of breaking a rule. They also say that if a person shows both signs, then we can say that the person is a law breaker. The speaker treats the first claim and the second claim as statements they believe are true.

The second idea is the step that the speaker thinks follows from those true statements. They think that because the first claim says “if act one then act two” and the second claim says “if act two then law breaking”, we can put the two together and say that if a person does act one, then we can be sure the person is a law breaker. In other words, they think the two “if … then …” parts link together to give a final result about the person.

The third idea looks at whether that step is a good one. The link the speaker makes works only if the two “if … then …” parts are both reliable in every case. In real life the first part is only likely, not certain, and the second part is also only likely, not certain. Because the speaker treats “likely” as if it were a guarantee, the step from the first act to calling the person a law breaker is too strong. So the thinking moves from “might happen” to “must happen”, which is not a good move. The conclusion can be wrong if either of the two middle steps fails, so the reasoning is not solid even though the speaker thinks it follows.

english_query: why some deals hold together and others fall apart
ugf_response: First, we see that a deal is a word between two or more people about what each will do. Then we notice that each person will try to keep the word when the word helps them get something they want or when they care about the other person, and this makes the deal stay. Next, we see that a deal can fall apart when one person thinks the word does not help them any more, or when they think the other person will not keep their word, because then they stop trying to keep the deal. Also, when the word is not clear, people can think they mean different things, and this can cause the deal to break. Finally, we understand that deals that have a clear give and take, that are important to each side, and that are watched by others tend to hold together, while deals that are not clear, not useful, or where one side does not trust the other tend to fall apart.

english_query: what makes someone happy in the deepest way, not just for a moment
ugf_response:
Person 1: I wonder what makes a person feel happy in a strong way, not only for a short time.  
Person 2: A strong happy feeling often comes when a person feels close to other people they love and care.  
Person 1: How does being close to others help a strong happy feeling?  
Person 2: When you spend time with people who care for you, you feel good and you can share happy, and that feeling can stay longer.  
Person 1: What about the things a person does? Can they add to that strong happy feeling?  
Person 2: Yes, doing things that fit why you do them, like helping a person you like or learning something new, can make the happy feeling grow and stay.  
Person 1: Does thinking about who you are play a part?  
Person 2: When you act in a way that fits who you are, you feel good in, and that good feeling can last a long time.  
Person 1: So the many parts of love, good work, and being who you are make a strong happy feeling that does not go away quickly?  
Person 2: Exactly, the many parts can keep the happy feeling strong even after the first fun moment is gone.

english_query: why being good at being a person is like being good at anything else
ugf_response:
Person 1: Why does being good at being a person feel like being good at anything else?  
Person 2: Because both need you to learn how to act, how to think, and how to feel in ways that work for you and for others.  
Person 1: How do we learn to act well as a person?  
Person 2: You watch other people, you try what they do, you see what works and what does not, and you keep trying again.  
Person 1: Is that the same as learning a skill like drawing or cooking?  
Person 2: Yes, it is. In drawing you watch lines, you copy, you practice, and you get better; in being a person you watch words, you copy kind ways, you practice, and you get better.  
Person 1: What makes the learning part work?  
Person 2: When you try and see a result, your brain makes a note of it. If the result feels good, you will try it more; if it feels bad, you will try a different way.  
Person 1: So the feeling of good or bad guides us?  
Person 2: Exactly. The feeling tells you what to keep and what to change, just like a teacher tells you when a picture looks right or wrong.  
Person 1: Does that mean anyone can get better at being a person?  
Person 2: Yes, anyone can keep watching, trying, and learning, and they will get better at being a person just like anyone can get better at any other thing.

english_query: why the rules should be the same for everyone
ugf_response:
In this case we have a rule that says everybody must do the same thing when a certain situation comes up. The rule is written down and the people who live in the place can see it on signs or hear it from the people who make the rules. When the rule is there, each person can look at it and then decide whether to follow it, to ask for a change, or to ignore it. The rule is the same for every single person, no matter how old they are, where they live, or what they do for work.  

The hard question that comes up is whether it is right for the rule to be the same for everyone. Some people think that a rule that is the same for all is the best way to be fair because it does not give any one person a special treat. They say that if the rule is the same, then no one can say that the rule is hurting them by being different from what others get. They feel that this kind of sameness protects people from being picked on or from being left out.  

Other people say that the rule should not be the same for all because people are not all the same. They point out that some people need extra help, some people have a hard time doing what the rule asks, and some people might be put in danger if they have to follow the rule in the same way as everyone else. For them, being fair means giving each person what they need, not giving each person the very same thing. They argue that a rule that does not change can hurt those who are already in a hard spot, and that a rule that can change to fit each person’s need would keep more people safe and happy.  

A third group says that the rule can be the same for most things, but there are special cases where it must bend. They think the rule should stay the same when it is about keeping people safe, like when a rule stops a fire or a storm, because in those moments the same action for all can stop a big problem. But they also think the rule can be different when it is about helping people with health, work, or school, because those areas need a softer touch.  

All of these answers come from the same idea that we want to protect people and make life as good as possible, but they look at the idea of “the same for all” in different ways. Some see sameness as the only way to keep things fair, some see sameness as a way to hurt those who are not like the rest, and some try to find a middle path that keeps the good parts of both views. The question stays open for each group to think about what they feel is the right way to treat everybody.

english_query: counterexample to: A person S is entitled to restitution from a person T if and only if (i) T has b
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person puts forward is a claim about when one person should have a right to get back something from another. They say that the first person, we can call them S, has a right to get back something from the second person, we can call them T, exactly when T has a thing we call b (and possibly when other things happen, but the claim focuses on that one point). In other words, the claim is that the presence of b in T is the only thing that decides whether S can ask for pay back.

The second idea is the example that the person brings up to try to show that the claim does not always work. They describe a situation where T does have the thing b, but S does not get any pay back, or a situation where S does get pay back even though T does not have b. By pointing out that the link between b and the right to pay back is broken in this situation, they say the original claim must be wrong. This example is meant to be a counter‑example that shows the rule does not hold for every case.

The third idea is the conclusion they draw from the example. Because the example seems to break the rule, they think we must change the rule or add more conditions so that it matches what really happens. This line of thinking is good if the example truly fits all the parts of the original claim. If the example really does have T with b and still S does not get pay back, then the claim that b alone decides the right is indeed false, and the move to look for a better rule is sensible. However, if the example leaves out some other condition that the original claim also required, then the example does not really break the claim, and the jump to say the whole rule is wrong would be a weak step. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the example fully matches the situation described in the first idea. If it does, the thinking is solid; if it does not, the thinking is not well supported.

english_query: material conditional, paradoxical — inductive
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being put forward is that the rule that says “if one thing is true then another thing is true” is a rule that always works when we talk about statements. In other words, the rule about “if … then …” never fails in the way we use it in plain talk, so that part of the case is true.  

The second idea that follows from this rule is that because the rule never fails, we can take a single example where the first part is true and then claim that the second part must be true for every case. This step makes a result that looks odd, because it seems to say that just one example can tell us about all other examples, which feels like a thing that goes against what we normally expect when we learn by looking at many cases.  

The third idea is an evaluation of the reasoning. The claim that the “if … then …” rule is always correct is right, but the move from one example to all examples is not a strong move. The rule works for statements, not for saying that what happens in one real situation must happen in every real situation. So the thinking that jumps from the first idea to the second idea is not a good one, because it uses a rule in a way that does not match how we usually gather knowledge from many observations.

english_query: counterexample to: Two or more agents cooperate if and only if (i) each agent is aware that they ar
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person gives is a story about a group of people who end up doing a joint action even though not every one of them knows that the other people are also part of that joint action. In the story one person helps a second person with a task. A third person sees the first person helping and also helps the second person, but the third person does not realize that the first person is also helping the same second person. The story says that the joint action still happens even when the knowledge that everyone else is also part of it is missing for at least one of the people.

The second idea that the person draws from that story is that the claim “people work together when and only when each person knows that they are working together” must be wrong. Because the story shows a case where the joint action occurs while the knowledge condition is not met, the person says the original claim cannot be true for all situations. In other words, the story is meant to be a proof that the knowledge condition is not needed for people to work together.

The third idea is an assessment of whether that line of thinking is sound. If the story really does show a joint action where at least one person lacks the knowledge of the others, then it does show that the knowledge condition is not required in every case, so the reasoning that the original claim is false is correct. The only way the reasoning could fail is if, on closer look, the third person actually does have some indirect way of knowing that the first person is also helping, or if the story does not really count as a joint action because the people are not aiming at the same goal. Assuming the story holds as described, the thinking that the original claim is too strong is good, because it points out a clear situation where the claim does not match what actually happens.

english_query: indicative conditional, constructive_dilemma — deontological
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being said is that a sentence that has the form “if something happens, then something else happens” is true. In other words, they say that when the first part of the sentence is true, the second part must also be true. They also add that when we have two of those sentences, we can put them together and get a new sentence that talks about two possible things that could happen.

The second idea that follows from the first is that because the “if‑then” sentences are true, we have a rule about what people must do. They say that when the first part of the first sentence is true, people must do the second part, and when the first part of the second sentence is true, people must do the second part of that one. They think this gives a kind of “must‑do” rule for both cases, a rule that does not change even if other things are going on.

The thinking behind the second idea is not completely solid. Just because a sentence that links two things is true does not by itself tell us that anyone has to act in a certain way. To turn a true link into a rule about what we must do, we need another reason, such as a belief that we have to follow rules no matter what. Without that extra reason, the step from the true “if‑then” sentence to the “must‑do” rule is a jump that is not fully supported. So the first part is true, the second part is what they think follows, but the thinking that moves from the first to the second is weak because it assumes that truth alone creates a rule for action.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument for God's existence is compelling if and only if (i) it proceeds fro
ugf_response: The first idea they put forward is that a talk that tries to show the big power is real can be strong only when it does a certain set of things. They say this point is true and that without those things the talk cannot be strong. The second idea they think follows from that claim is that because the talk they look at does not do those things, it must not be strong. In other words, they see a case where the talk is missing the required parts and then say that this case shows the rule they stated does not work. The third part is an evaluation of the thinking. The thinking is clear in that it follows a simple pattern: look at the rule, see a case that does not fit the rule, and then say the rule must be wrong. This can be a good way to test a rule, but it can also miss other ways the talk might be strong that do not need the exact set of things the rule mentions. So the thinking is useful for pointing out a problem, but it may not be enough to decide for sure whether the whole idea about what makes a talk strong is right or not.

english_query: what it means for a case to be put together well
ugf_response:
The first idea says that a case that is put together well has parts that are clear and that each part shows a fact that is real. It also says that the parts line up with each other so that the whole thing looks like one smooth picture. The speaker treats these statements as true because we can see when a case is clear and when the parts fit like a puzzle.

The second idea says that because the parts are clear and fit, the case will make people think the main say is right. In other words, the speaker thinks that a clear and well‑lined case will cause the listeners to accept the main say without doubt. The speaker believes that the good shape of the case is enough to make the listeners follow the main say.

When we look at the thinking we see that the first idea is a good observation – clear parts that fit do help people understand a case. The second idea, however, goes a little farther than the first idea tells us. Just because a case looks clear does not automatically make the main say true; the facts behind the parts also have to be true. So the thinking has a good part in seeing the value of clear parts, but it is not fully good because it leaves out the need to check that the facts themselves are right.

english_query: anomaly analogy (predictive) — mechanical_and_repair
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is that there is a thing that does not fit the normal pattern in one situation, and that this thing looks a lot like a different thing that can happen with machines when we try to fix them. The second idea they then say is that because the two things look alike, we can use the first one to guess what will happen next in the machine fixing case. The third idea they add is that this guess can help us decide what to do before the problem gets bigger.

When we look at the first idea, the claim that the odd case really does look like the machine fixing case can be true if the details match up well. It is possible for a strange event in one area to share the same shape as a problem in another area, so the statement can be correct. The second idea, that the similarity lets us make a guess about the future, follows the usual way people think: if two things are alike, what happens to one may happen to the other. This step is a common way of thinking, but it only works well when the two things share the important parts that cause the result. The third idea, that the guess can help us act early, follows from the second idea only if the guess is reliable.

To see if the thinking is good, we have to ask whether the odd case and the machine fixing case share the key parts that make them behave the way they do. If they share those parts, then using the odd case to guess is a useful step. If they only look alike on the surface, then the guess may be weak and could lead us to the wrong action. So the reasoning can be good when the comparison is deep and the parts that cause the outcome are the same, but it can be poor when the comparison is only shallow. In short, the claim can be true, the follow‑on guess can follow from it, and the quality of the thinking depends on how strong the hidden match is between the two situations.

english_query: counterexample to: A person A has an obligation to perform action X if and only if (i) A has volunt
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being said is that a person A must do a certain thing X only when two things happen: first, A does the thing because they want to, and second, some other condition that the speaker calls “voluntary” is also true. The words “only when” mean that if one of those two things is not true, then A does not have to do X. The speaker says this part is true, and it is easy to see that it can be true in many situations. For instance, if a child decides to clean their room because they want to and also because they are not being forced, then we can agree that the child must clean the room in that case.

The second idea the speaker tries to get from the first idea is that whenever the two things are true, A is forced to do X. In other words, the speaker thinks that the two conditions are enough to make A have to do the thing. The speaker says that because the first idea is true, the second idea must follow, so they think the rule works for every case.

The thinking that links the two ideas is not solid. A case can be made where the two conditions are true but A still does not have to do X. Imagine a person A who wants to give a gift to a friend and also is not being forced by anyone, yet the friend already has the same gift. In that case the two conditions are true, but A does not have to give the gift because it would not change anything. This shows that the step from the first idea to the second idea does not always work. The reasoning assumes that the two conditions are enough, but the case shows that there can be other factors that stop A from being forced to do X. So the first idea may be correct, but the move to the second idea is not a good one because it leaves out other important parts that can stop the requirement.

english_query: why who someone is to you changes what you should do for them
ugf_response: First, we think about the way we are close to a person. Then we see that being close makes us feel a certain way about how we act. If the person is a child we may need to keep them safe and give them food and love. If the person is a friend we may want to share time, have fun and help when they need it. If the person is a boss we may need to listen to what they say and do the work they ask for. This happens because each kind of person has different needs and because we have different things we need to do for them. So the place a person has in our life changes the kind of help, care and action we should give them.

english_query: counterexample to: A region has a particular climate if and only if (i) the average temperature and
ugf_response:
The first idea that is put forward is a story about a certain area and the way its weather is measured. In that story the speaker says that the area has a certain kind of weather pattern when the middle hotness and the middle wetness of the year have some particular values, and also that if the area has that kind of weather pattern then those middle hotness and wetness values must be there. The speaker then shows a real case where the middle hotness and wetness are exactly those values but the area does not have the kind of weather pattern that the rule says it should have. This part of the story is true in the sense that the numbers are correct and the weather that is observed is also correct, so the example does match the description of the rule’s first half but not its second half.

The second idea that the speaker tries to pull from that story is that because the real case does not fit the rule, the rule itself must be wrong. The speaker says that the rule that ties the weather pattern to the two middle numbers cannot be true for all places, because here is a place that breaks it. In other words the speaker thinks that one case that does not follow the rule is enough to show that the rule does not hold in general.

The third idea is an evaluation of that line of thought. It is good to check a rule by looking for a case that does not fit, and finding one such case does indeed show that the rule cannot be true for every place. However, the thinking would be stronger if the speaker also explained why the rule was thought to be true in the first place, and whether the rule was meant to apply to every possible place or only to places that meet some other hidden condition. As it stands, the speaker’s claim that the rule is false is supported by the example, so the thinking is solid, but the overall argument would be clearer if it also mentioned any limits that might have been meant for the rule.

english_query: how to find the hidden parts of someone's case
ugf_response: First, we pay attention to what the person says and how they act in simple ways. Then we watch how they act when no one is looking, because this can show parts that are not spoken. Next, we ask the person gentle questions that let them talk about why they do things, and we listen for small signs in their answers. After that, we talk to other people who know the person, such as friends or family, and we ask them what they have seen. Then we look at things the person keeps, like notes or pictures, and we think about what those things might tell us about hidden parts of their story. Finally, we put all the small signs together in our mind and see a bigger picture that shows the hidden parts of the person's story. This way we can find the parts that are not obvious at first.

english_query: counterexample to: A region R is a space if and only if (i) R is bounded — that is, R has limits or
ugf_response: The first idea that is being talked about is a rule that says a place is a kind of area when and only when it has limits, that is, when it does not go on forever in any direction. The second idea that the person brings is a case that they say fits the rule in a different way: they point to a place that does have limits but still does not act like the kind of area the rule talks about, or they point to a place that does not have limits but still seems to work as that kind of area. The third idea is the claim that because this case exists, the rule cannot be true in every situation. In other words, they think that the rule must be wrong because they have found a place that does not follow it. The fourth idea is the judgment about whether the thinking is good. The case itself can be checked and it is true that the place they chose does have the features they say it has, so the description of the case is correct. However, the step that says the rule is then false depends on whether the rule was meant to apply to every possible place or only to a certain kind of place. If the rule was meant to be a universal claim, then finding one place that does not fit does indeed show the rule is not always right, and the thinking is good. If the rule was only meant for a narrower group of places, then the case does not really break the rule, and the thinking would be a mistake. So the truth of the first part is solid, the step that the rule must be wrong follows only if the rule was meant to be universal, and the quality of the reasoning is good only when that condition is met.

english_query: abstraction analogy (heuristic) — mechanical_and_repair
ugf_response:
The first idea that the speaker says is true is that we can look at a problem the way we look at a broken machine. When we see a machine that does not work, we can take it apart, see which part is not moving right, and then try to fix that part. The speaker claims that the same way of looking at a problem – by thinking of it as a set of parts that can be taken apart – is something we can really do, and that claim matches what many people actually do when they try to understand a hard thing.

The second idea that the speaker says follows from the first is that, because we can look at a problem like a broken machine, we can also use the same steps we use to fix a machine to solve the problem. In other words, the speaker thinks that if we first find the part that is not working, then we can change or replace that part, and the whole thing will work again. The speaker also adds a third idea that using this way of looking at things will help us find answers faster and will make the work of solving problems easier.

The thinking behind these ideas is partly good and partly weak. It is good that the first idea matches a real way that people break down hard things into smaller parts, and that the first idea is a true observation. It is also useful that the second idea gives a simple way to act – look for a broken part and try to fix it – because that can often lead to a quick fix. However, the jump from the first idea to the third idea is not always right. Not every problem works like a machine, and some problems have parts that cannot be taken apart or fixed in the same way. So the belief that the same steps will always work can be a bad guess when the problem is more about ideas or feelings than about parts that can be swapped. In short, the first claim is true, the second claim follows in many cases, and the overall thinking is useful as a simple guide but should not be taken as a rule that works for every kind of problem.

english_query: what it means to live a good life by being a good person
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is that a life that feels good is the same as being a person who does good things. They say this part is true and that the good feeling of life comes from the good way the person acts. The second idea they add is that because the first part is true, if a person tries to be good then they will have a life that feels good. In other words they think the good way of acting will bring the good feeling of life, so they think the first part makes the second part happen.

When we look at the link between the two ideas we can ask if the step makes sense. It can make sense in many cases because doing good can bring help from other people, can bring a feeling of calm, and can keep a person from hurting others, all of which can add to a feeling that life is good. But it does not always fit together. A person can do good and still have hard luck, be sick, or lose things that make life feel bad. A person can also feel good in life even if they do not act in a good way, for example if they get a lot of help from others or have a lot of things that make life easy. Because the second idea says that the first idea always leads to the good feeling of life, the thinking leaves out the times when the link is weak or missing. So the step from the first idea to the second idea is only partly right; it works in many situations but it does not work in all. The overall thinking is therefore not completely solid – it has a good point that good actions can help make life feel good, but it also leaves out other things that can change how life feels, so the thinking could be made stronger by adding those missing parts.

ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is that there is a rule about the way we put things in groups. The rule says that the way we put things in groups is good for study when it has a certain part that we will call the first part. The person says that this rule is true for all cases.

The second idea that the person says follows from the first idea is that they have a case that does not have the first part but still seems to work as a group. Because this case does not have the first part, the person says that the rule must be wrong. They say that their case shows the rule does not work for every thing.

The third idea is the way we judge the thinking. If the rule really says it works for every single thing, then one case that does not fit is enough to show the rule is not right, so the thinking can be good. But if the rule was meant to work only for things that have the first part, then the case does not belong to the set the rule talks about, and the thinking would be weak. In other words, the person’s claim that the rule is wrong depends on whether their case truly belongs to the same kind of thing the rule talks about. If it does, the thinking is solid; if it does not, the thinking is not strong.

english_query: abstraction analogy (heuristic) — epistemology
ugf_response: The first idea that the person says is that when we try to understand how we know what we know, it can help to take away many small details and look at a simple picture that shows the main parts. This claim is partly true because a simple picture can make a hard thing easier to see and can show a pattern that is hidden in a lot of small parts. The second idea they add is that because that simple picture works for one kind of knowing, it must work for every kind of knowing, and that the picture can be used as a rule for all other times we try to see if something is true. The thinking here is that the success of the simple picture in one place should be taken as a sign that it is a good rule for all places. The third idea is the judge of the thinking: the step from one case to all cases is not a safe step. It is a jump that needs more checking, because a picture that fits one set of details may miss something important in another set. So the reasoning is not fully good; it uses a helpful tool, but it assumes the tool works everywhere without testing it, and that makes the argument weaker than it could be.

english_query: counterexample to: A person has broken a promise if and only if (i) the person uttered words to the
ugf_response:
The first idea is a statement that says a person has broken a promise just when they said some words that made the promise. This statement tries to say that the only way a promise can be broken is if the promise was first spoken. That part is not true in all cases because people can make promises without using words, for example by a look, a gift, or a sign, and they can also break a promise that was spoken in a way that does not fit the exact wording the statement requires. So the first idea does not hold for every situation.

The second idea is an example that tries to show the problem with the first idea. In the example a person nods and gives a small gift that everyone understands as a promise to help later. Later the person does not help. In this case the promise was not spoken, yet it was still broken. The example is true in the sense that the situation can happen and it shows a case where a promise is broken without any spoken words.

The third idea is the step that says because the example exists, the original statement must be wrong. This step follows the usual rule that one example that does not fit the rule is enough to show the rule is not always correct. Since the example really does show a broken promise without spoken words, the step that the original statement is false is a good step.

The fourth idea is a judgment about how good the whole thinking is. The thinking starts with a claim that is too narrow, then gives a clear case that does not fit the claim, and finally says the claim must be wrong. Because the case is real and matches what the claim says it cannot handle, the reasoning is solid. If the case were not really a promise, the reasoning would be weaker, but as it stands the chain of thought is sound.

english_query: how doing good things over and over makes you into a good person
ugf_response: First, we see that when a person does a nice thing the person feels good and the other people see it and may feel good too. Then we can see that if the person does more nice things again and again the person gets used to doing them and it becomes part of how the person does. This means that the more many times a person does nice things the more the person will think about doing them and the more the person will be seen by others as a nice person. Then we notice that other people may copy the nice things and that makes the world have more nice things which makes the first person feel even more good about being nice. This shows that doing nice things many times can change the way a person thinks about them and can make the person into a nice person.

english_query: why we should not accept something just because many people believe it
ugf_response: First, we see that many people can think the same thing. Then we see that just because many think it, it does not mean it is the right one. This is because each person can be not right in a different way. When a lot of people think the same, they can push each other to keep that think even if it does not work with what we can see or what we know from trying. This means we need to look at what we can see and try it out for our own head. When we try it we can see if it works. If it works, then we can be sure it is right. If it does not work, then we know the many people were not right. So we should not just take a think because many think it; we should use our head and what we see to choose.

english_query: strict conditional, modus_ponens — evidential
ugf_response: The first idea that is being said as true is a rule that says if one thing is true then another thing is also true, and that rule is meant to work all the time without any exception. The person also says that they have the first thing, so they can use that rule to get the second thing. The second idea is that from the first idea and the rule they think they can get a new piece of what can be taken as proof that the second thing is true. They are using the rule that if we know a rule that says if A then B, and we also know A, then we can say B, and they say that this move gives us a piece of proof about B. The third idea is about whether this way of moving from the first idea to the second is a good way to get proof. The thinking is good in the sense that the rule they are using works for statements that are meant to be always true, so if the first statement really does fit that kind of rule, then getting the second statement is a correct step. However, if the first statement is not really a rule that works all the time, or if the rule is being used for something that is only sometimes true, then the step does not give a reliable piece of proof. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the original rule is truly of the always‑true kind; when it is, the move is solid, and when it is not, the move does not give a trustworthy result.

english_query: the idea that the right thing to do is what leads to the most good
ugf_response: First, we think about what we call good. Good is the feeling that people have when something helps them or makes them happy or safe. Then we think about actions. An action is what a person does. Each action can make some good happen and can also make some not good happen. Next, we try to see which action makes the most good. To do that we can look at how many people feel helped, happy, or safe because of the action. The action that makes the biggest amount of that feeling is the one that gives the most good. Finally, we say that the best thing to do is the thing that gives the most good. In this way we choose actions by looking at how much good they bring to people, and we pick the one that brings the most. This helps us decide what to do when we want to act in a way that helps the most.
